original_abstract: This paper explores the implementation of bilingual Czech-Polish nomenclature across the railway network of the Zaolzie region. By assessing the current socio-cultural standing of the Polish national minority, the study examines the role of key advocacy groups—most notably the Congress of Poles in the Czech Republic—in promoting linguistic diversity. While the research outlines the theoretical and legal frameworks governing bilingualism in the Czech Republic, its primary focus is a practical analysis of how bilingual signage has been introduced at railway stations. Beyond physical signage, the study investigates the broader use of the Polish language in public transit, including its integration into timetables and station announcements. Finally, the paper highlights the persistent challenges and systemic obstacles that continue to hinder the full realization of bilingualism in the region’s transport infrastructure.
humanized_abstract: Abstract: AbstractIn the paper, we focus on the implementation of bilingual Czech–Polish nomenclature on the railways in the Zaolzie region. We analyze the socio-cultural position of the Polish national minority in Czech part of Zaolzie region and we describe the role of some important civic associations, such as Congress of Poles in the Czech Republic, which promote the Czech language as well as the Polish and German minority languages in the Czech Republic. In the practical part, we focus on the legislation and theories in relation to bilingualism in the Czech Republic and we analyze the implementation of bilingual nomenclature on some railway stations. We also focused on the use of Polish language in public transport—displaying it on timetables and on electronic announcements. In the conclusion, we summarize the obstacles which still prevent effective implementation of bilingual nomenclature in transport infrastructure in Zaolzie region.

In the practical part, we focus on the legislation and theories in relation to bilingualism in the Czech Republic and we analyze the implementation of bilingual nomenclature on some railway stations. We also focused on the use of Polish language in public transport—displaying it on timetables and on electronic announcements. In the conclusion, we summarize the obstacles which still prevent effective implementation of bilingual nomenclature in transport infrastructure in Zaolzie region.
